mcqs.question: Which of the following is TRUE about threads compared to processes?

options.text: Threads share the same address space within a process
Creating a thread requires allocating a new address space
Threads cannot communicate with each other directly
Each thread has its own independent heap memory

mcqs.explanation: Threads within the same process share the address space, code section, data section, and other resources. This is why they are called lightweight processes. Creating a thread is faster than creating a process because it doesn't require a new address space.

mcqs.question: What is the main advantage of user-level threads over kernel-level threads?

options.text: They can run on multiprocessor systems
They are faster to create and manage
The OS scheduler manages them directly
They provide true parallelism

mcqs.explanation: User-level threads are managed entirely in user space by a thread library, making them faster to create, switch, and manage. However, they cannot directly utilize multiple processors.

mcqs.question: In which thread model can multiple user threads run in parallel on a multiprocessor system?

options.text: Many-to-one model
One-to-one model
One-to-many model
Many-to-many model

mcqs.explanation: The one-to-one model maps each user thread to a kernel thread, allowing the OS to schedule multiple threads on different processors simultaneously, enabling true parallelism.

mcqs.question: Which data structure is typically used to represent a thread control block (TCB)?

options.text: Stack
Queue
Linked list or structure
Array

mcqs.explanation: A Thread Control Block (TCB) is a data structure, typically implemented as a structure or linked list entry, that stores information about a thread including thread ID, program counter, register values, stack pointer, and thread state.

mcqs.question: What happens when the main thread in a program terminates?

options.text: All other threads are immediately terminated
The entire process continues until all threads finish
The process terminates and takes all threads with it
Only user-level threads are affected

mcqs.explanation: When the main thread (or any thread) calls exit() or when the main thread terminates in some systems, the entire process terminates, and all its threads are destroyed regardless of their state.

mcqs.question: Which of the following is NOT a benefit of multithreading?

options.text: Improved CPU utilization
Simplified program structure
Enhanced responsiveness
Efficient communication between processes

mcqs.explanation: Multithreading provides efficient communication within a process (between threads) using shared memory. Communication between separate processes still requires IPC mechanisms. So efficient inter-process communication is not a direct benefit of multithreading.

mcqs.question: In the many-to-many threading model, what happens if a user thread blocks?

options.text: All associated user threads block
Only the blocking user thread is affected
The kernel thread it is bound to continues running
The entire process blocks

mcqs.explanation: In the many-to-many model, if one user thread blocks (e.g., for I/O), the kernel can schedule another user thread to run on that kernel thread. This prevents the entire application from blocking.

mcqs.question: What is thread pooling primarily used for?

options.text: To create more threads when needed
To limit the number of active threads and reuse them
To synchronize threads
To increase thread priority

mcqs.explanation: Thread pooling creates a pool of pre-created threads that are kept ready to execute tasks from a queue. This avoids the overhead of creating and destroying threads for each task, improving performance.

mcqs.question: Which of the following is TRUE about kernel-level threads?

options.text: They are managed entirely in user space
Context switching between them is slower than user-level threads
The OS kernel is aware of and schedules them directly
They cannot be preempted by the OS scheduler

mcqs.explanation: Kernel-level threads are directly managed by the operating system kernel. The kernel handles thread creation, scheduling, and management. Context switching between kernel threads requires kernel intervention.

mcqs.question: What is the main difference between parallelism and concurrency?

options.text: Parallelism is only for single-core systems
Concurrency involves multiple threads on one CPU
Parallelism requires multiple CPUs or cores
There is no difference between them

mcqs.explanation: Parallelism refers to simultaneous execution on multiple processing units (multiple cores/CPUs), while concurrency refers to managing multiple tasks that can make progress over overlapping time periods (can occur on single CPU through time-sharing).

mcqs.question: What does the program counter of a thread store?
mcqs.address: The location of the next instruction to be executed

options.text: The memory address of the thread's stack
The location of the next instruction to be executed
The thread's priority level
The thread's current state flags

mcqs.explanation: Each thread has its own program counter (PC) that tracks the address of the next instruction to execute. This allows threads to execute independently and be context-switched at any point.

mcqs.question: Which thread library is commonly used for POSIX-compliant systems?

options.text: Java Thread API
Python threading module
pthread (POSIX threads)
Windows Thread API

mcqs.explanation: pthread (POSIX threads) is the standardized thread API for POSIX-compliant UNIX and Linux systems. It provides functions like pthread_create(), pthread_join(), pthread_mutex_lock() for thread management and synchronization.

mcqs.question: What is a race condition in multithreaded programs?

options.text: When multiple threads compete for CPU time
When the outcome depends on the timing of thread execution
When threads wait for each other indefinitely
When a thread exits unexpectedly

mcqs.explanation: A race condition occurs when the program's outcome depends on the relative execution order of multiple threads accessing shared data. This is a critical synchronization issue that can lead to incorrect results.

mcqs.question: Which of the following is a valid thread state in a typical thread life cycle?

options.text: Suspended
Zombie
Ready
Sleeping

mcqs.explanation: The typical thread states are NEW, READY, RUNNING, BLOCKED/WAITING, and TERMINATED. Ready state indicates the thread is waiting in the ready queue to be scheduled for execution.

mcqs.question: What is thread cancellation in operating systems?

options.text: Stopping a thread after it completes its task normally
Terminating a thread before it completes execution
Pausing a thread temporarily
Changing thread priority

mcqs.explanation: Thread cancellation is the process of terminating a thread before it has completed its execution. It can be cooperative (thread checks cancellation request) or asynchronous (OS terminates immediately).

mcqs.question: What is the primary purpose of thread synchronization primitives like mutexes?

options.text: To increase thread execution speed
To prevent race conditions on shared resources
To create new threads faster
To increase CPU utilization

mcqs.explanation: Mutexes and other synchronization primitives are used to prevent race conditions by ensuring mutual exclusion when multiple threads access shared resources. Only one thread can hold the mutex at a time.

mcqs.question: Which statement is TRUE about daemon threads in Java?

options.text: Daemon threads always complete before the program exits
The JVM does not wait for daemon threads to finish before exiting
Daemon threads have higher priority than user threads
Daemon threads cannot access shared resources

mcqs.explanation: In Java, the JVM terminates automatically when all user (non-daemon) threads finish execution, regardless of whether daemon threads are still running. The JVM does not wait for daemon threads to complete.

mcqs.question: What is the main challenge in multithreaded programming?

options.text: Creating too few threads
Thread synchronization and avoiding race conditions
Assigning too much memory to threads
Scheduling threads fairly

mcqs.explanation: The main challenge in multithreaded programming is properly synchronizing threads to avoid race conditions, deadlocks, and other concurrency issues while ensuring correct program behavior and performance.

mcqs.question: Which of the following is an example of a thread-specific resource?

options.text: Global variables
Open files
Stack and registers
Heap memory

mcqs.explanation: Each thread has its own stack and register set (including program counter). These are thread-specific resources. Other resources like global variables, open files, and heap memory are shared among threads in the same process.

mcqs.question: What is the relationship between a process and its threads?

options.text: A process can exist without any threads
Every process must have exactly one thread
A process must have at least one thread (the main thread)
Threads and processes are unrelated concepts

mcqs.explanation: A process must have at least one thread, called the main thread, to execute instructions. Additional threads can be created within the process. When the last thread terminates, the process terminates.
